segments.s210-g105: اگر begins a conditional clause ('if'). من is the pronoun subject. شوم is the 1st‑person present-subjunctive of شدن used here with the past participle کُشته to form a passive ('be killed'); in Classical Persian the past participle (کشته) often precedes the auxiliary (شوم). بر دست طوس is an agent/means phrase meaning 'by the hand of Tūs' (بر + noun 'دست' with the possessor طوس); word order places the conditional verb cluster before the adverbial agent.
segments.s210-g106: نه negates the following verb. برخیزد is 3rd‑person singular present‑subjunctive/optative (here passive/inchoative in sense 'be raised' or 'arise') and precedes its subject, a common poetic order. آیین is the nominal head 'rite/ceremony' and گوپال و کوس are coordinated nouns linked by و ('and'); together they form the subject phrase 'the rites/signals of the gūpāl and the drum' which 'will not be raised/sounded.'
segments.s210-g107: بجایست is an impersonal, archaic/polite predicate meaning 'it is fitting/appropriate' and introduces what follows as desirable; it is followed here by the nominative subject/pivot 'پیران و افراسیاب' (the elders and Afrasiab). Word order is predicate → subject, a common classical Persian inversion for emphasis.
segments.s210-g108: بخواهد is a 3rd-person singular form meaning 'would wish/wants' that governs a subordinate verbal clause. شدن is used here as a light verb meaning 'to be / to befall' (i.e. 'to be shed / to happen'), and خون من is the affected noun phrase 'my blood.' رود is the 3rd-person form of a verb 'to go/flow' (here 'flow away like water') followed by آب as the simile 'like water.' Word order is somewhat compressed: the main verb 'بخواهد' introduces the desiderative clause whose event is expressed by شدن + nominal object + verbal phrase 'رود آب.'
segments.s210-g109: نه is a negating/contrasting particle ('no' / 'not so') starting the clause. گیتی (the world) is the subject and شود (become) is the verb; پاک here functions adverbially ('utterly/entirely') modifying ویران ('ruined, desolate'). ز من is a poetic prepositional phrase 'on account of me' or 'because of me.' The overall clause denies that the world will be wholly ruined for the speaker's sake.
segments.s210-g110: This is an inverted order of the necessity construction: normally بِاید + verb, but here 'سخن راند باید' means 'one must speak' (سخن راندن = to speak/utter). بدین انجمن is 'to this assembly' formed from the prepositional prefix ب + (demonstrative) دین + انجمن; the phrase as a whole marks the target/venue of speaking. Note: the particle 'ب' is the preposition 'to/into' and 'دین' here is part of the demonstrative 'بدین' ('to this'), so the unit means 'to this assembly.'
segments.s210-g111: وگر is the conditional 'if.' طوس (Tūs) is the subject and گردد (a form of شدن/گشتن) is the verb 'become / happen' used here for a hypothetical situation. بدستم is the contracted prepositional phrase 'به دستِ من' meaning 'into my hands,' and تباه is a predicate adjective 'ruined/destroyed.' The clause therefore says 'if Tūs should be ruined in my hands.'
segments.s210-g112: یکی here functions as 'not one / not a single' in combination with the negative-subjunctive verb نیابند (classical subjunctive/optative 'may not find' / 'will not find'). ره is 'way' or 'route.' ز ایران سپاه is a poetic order meaning 'the host/army of Iran' (ز = 'of/from' in poetic usage). So the clause states that not a single member of the Iranian host will find a way/route.
segments.s210-g249: This is a fronted indirect object + reporting verb + subject followed by the conditional introducer. بدو (to him) precedes گفت (said) — Persian permits the indirect object before the verb. گفت is the past reporting verb introducing the following subordinate clause. گودرز is the speaker's name placed after the verb (a common poetic word order). اگر begins the conditional clause that follows.
segments.s210-g250: بگرداند is a third-person singular (optative/subjunctive) form of گرداند used after اگر to express a potential/hypothetical action (“should cause/bring”). از ما functions as a prepositional phrase 'upon/against us' with از before the pronoun ما. بد روزگار is a noun phrase where the adjective بد precedes روزگار (typical Persian order), meaning 'ill fortune' or 'bad times'; together they complete the conditional clause.
segments.s210-g251: به بیشی و کمی is a prepositional phrase meaning 'with regard to more and less' (i.e. 'of greater or lesser'). نباشد is a negative jussive/subjunctive form meaning 'let there not be' or 'there should be no'; it precedes سخن (speech/talk), so the whole phrase means 'let there be no talk of more or less.' The verb+noun order here is normal for giving a general prohibition in poetry.
segments.s210-g252: دل و مغز ایرانیان is a coordinated noun phrase 'the heart and core/brain of the Iranians' with the genitive ایرانیان following. بد مکن is a negative imperative: بد (bad/ill) qualifies the action and مکن is the negative imperative 'do not (do)'; together 'do not make [them] bad' i.e. 'do not sadden/distress.' The object (دل و مغز ایرانیان) is understood as the target of مکن.
segments.s210-g253: اگر introduces a conditional. بد بود is an adjectival predicate with the past copula بود ('was' / subjunctive sense here for a hypothetical): 'were bad/poor.' بخشش آسمان is a genitive noun phrase 'the grant/forgiveness of heaven' (بخشش = grant/donation here) serving as the subject/predicate complement; the clause reads 'if the heavens' grant were poor.'
segments.s210-g254: بپرهیز is an imperative meaning 'beware' or 'refrain' (a direct instruction). The conjunction و links this injunction to the next clause. The order بیشی نگردد زمان places the adverb/quantifier بیشی ('more/additional') before the negative optative نگردد ('may not become' / 'let not become') with زمان ('time') as the subject; literally 'and may time not become more' — i.e. 'and let time not increase.'
segments.s210-g255: تو is the emphatic subject pronoun 'you.' لشکر بیارای is an imperative/command 'array or marshal the army' — بیارای is an archaic/poetic verb form meaning 'array/arrange' (verb sense not supplied in the given senses). و links to the following clause. از بودنی is a prepositional phrase 'from that which is / out of the existent' where بودنی is a deverbal adjective/noun 'that-which-is' (existing things).
segments.s210-g256: روان را uses را as the definite/direct-object marker attached to روان ('soul/spirit'), so 'the souls/spirits' is the object. مکن is the negative imperative 'do not.' هیچ is an intensifier 'at all/anything.' بشخودنی is an archaic/obscure verb form (lemma بشخودن) given here as the verb to be negated; grammatically it functions as the infinitival/verbal complement of مکن ('do not [do] ...'), so the clause reads 'do not at all [do X] to the souls.' The precise lexical sense of بشخودنی is rare/archaic and not fully specified in the provided senses.
segments.s210-g145: This is a quantified nominal phrase: همه (a universal quantifier) scopes over two coordinated noun phrases joined by و. پرخاک is an adjective-like compound meaning “full of dust” (پر = “full (of)”), so کام پرخاک and پر خاک سر are parallel descriptions (‘mouths full of dust’ and ‘heads full of dust’); word order is modifier after the noun in this poetic line. The phrase is the semantic subject/background that the verb گرفتند will refer back to.
segments.s210-g146: گرفتند is a past transitive verb (third person plural) meaning ‘they seized/took’ and closes the clause begun in the previous segment. هر دو is an emphatic expression ‘both’; دوال is a poetic/archaic numeral/adjective meaning ‘twofold/pair’ modifying کمر (waist/girdle). The object of گرفتند is دوال کمر (they seized the waists/girdles), so the sequence has subject (implied from context/previous NP) + verb + object.
segments.s210-g147: ز is the poetic preposition ‘by/from’ introducing the cause phrase. نیروی گردان is a genitive/grouping ‘the force of the troop/massed ranks’ (نیروی = force/strength; گردان = troop). گران شد is a two-word predicate where شد is the past of شدن ‘to become’ and گران ‘heavy’ is the predicate adjective: ‘became heavy/were weighed down.’ رکیب is the affected person (the rider/companion) and functions as the logical subject of شدن.
segments.s210-g148: این جمله به صورت منفی بیان شده: یکی را is a marked object/topic ‘one (of them)’ with the object-marker را for emphasis; نیامد is the simple past negative meaning ‘did not come/was not able to.’ سر اندر نشیب is a prepositional/locative phrase (سر = ‘head’, اندر = ‘into/in’, نشیب = ‘descent/slope’). Together the clause means the one could not put/bring his head down into the descent (i.e. could not lower himself).
segments.s210-g149: This clause is coordinated: کمربند (belt/girth) is the subject; بگسست is a past intransitive verb with the prefix بـ (a classical/poetic perfective marker here) meaning ‘broke apart/snapped.’ و joins the following clause. هومان is a proper name (subject of the next verb) and بجست is past of جستن used here in the sense ‘leapt/sprang up.’
segments.s210-g150: یکی is an indefinite/numberal ‘one/a certain one’; اسپ ‘steed/horse’ is the object being taken. آسوده modifies اسپ and means ‘freed/at ease’ (here: the horse that is now free/loose). را is the definite object marker. برنشست is a classical compound verb (بر + نشستن) in the past: ‘sat upon/mounted.’ The clause therefore means someone mounted the now-free horse.
segments.s210-g151: سپهبد (spahbed) is the nominative agent here. سوی is the preposition ‘toward’; ترک + ش is written as two items in the segmentation: ترک ‘Turk’ and ش a enclitic possessive ‘his,’ so ترکیب yields ‘his Turk’ (i.e. the Turk under his command or his opponent). آورد is the past of آوردن ‘brought’ used here with a concrete motion meaning ‘brought/made’ and introduces the action; چنگ is an instrument noun ‘claw/hand’ (here figuratively ‘with his hand’ or ‘he struck’). Together this means the spahbed made a stroke/took action toward his Turk.
segments.s210-g152: This clause lists two actions coordinated by و. کمان را uses the object marker را after کمان ‘bow’; the verb بزه کرد is an older/poetic verbal phrase whose exact lexical sense is rare here (in context it means something like ‘handled/drew/struck with’ the bow) — the supplied senses list does not include this reading. کرد is the auxiliary/past ‘did/made.’ و links to تیر خدنگ: تیر ‘arrow’ is the object and خدنگ is an epithet (‘dagger-like’ or ‘short/pointed’), so the whole clause describes preparing/using the bow and sending a dagger-like arrow.
segments.s210-g33: تو‌گر is a fused poetic conditional meaning 'if you' (تو + اگر). پهلوانی is a nominal predicate 'a champion/hero' attached to that condition. ز قلب سپاه is a prepositional phrase 'from the heart/core of the army' where ز (poetic 'from') governs قلب سپاه (قلب as 'core' of سپاه 'army'); word order (preposition + noun + genitival noun) is typical for classical Persian.
segments.s210-g34: چرا is the interrogative 'why'. آمدستی is an archaic second-person singular past form ('thou camest' / 'you came') used here to ask why the person has come. بدین is written as the preposition ب + demonstrative/directional دین ('to/into this'), so ب functions as 'to/toward' and دین supplies the demonstrative sense 'this' (the provided sense list for دین does not include that meaning, so it is indicated below as unlisted). رزمگاه is the destination 'battlefield'.
segments.s210-g35: This line uses a compact classical order where the nominative descriptor (خردمند 'the wise man') appears first, followed by the predicate/complement (بیگانه 'a stranger') and then the verb خواند 'called'. ترا is the enclitic object 'you' (ت + را) placed after the verb for emphasis — in prose it would often appear before the verb. خواند here is used in the sense 'to call/name'.
segments.s210-g36: Parallel structure to the previous segment: هشیوار ('sane'/'alert') is the agent/nomee, دیوانه ('madman/mad') is the predicate applied to the addressee, خواند again is past 'called', and ترا (ت + را) is the second-person object clitic. The juxtaposition highlights contrasting judgments by different kinds of people.
segments.s210-g37: تو شو is an imperative 'be thou' (تو + شو 'become'). اختر کاویانی is a titular noun phrase 'Kayanian star' (اختر 'star' modified by کاویانی 'Kavian/Kayanian'). بدار here functions as a suffixal/participial element meaning 'holder/possessor' (‑دار), so the whole phrase reads 'be the Kayanian star-holder' — an archaic/poetic formation rather than the verb 'to hang' or the simple adjective 'awake'.
segments.s210-g38: سپهبد is the title 'spahbed' (general). نیاید is an older negative-present/negative-subjunctive form meaning 'does not come/does not appear'. سوی is the preposition 'toward' or 'to', and کارزار is 'battlefield' or 'battle'. Together they state that the spahbed does not come to the battlefield.
segments.s210-g39: نگه کن is an imperative phrase 'look!' or 'beware/observe' (نگه 'gaze/watch' + کن imperative 'do'). که introduces the following clause as a content clause ('that' / 'for'). خلعت is 'robe of honour' (object). کرا is an archaic relative/indefinite pronoun 'who/whom' (used here as 'to whom'), داد is the past verb 'gave', and شاه is the subject 'the king'. Word order is inverted poetically (object + indirect object + verb + subject).
segments.s210-g40: ز گردان is 'from the ranks/troops' (ز 'from' + گردان 'troop'). که introduces a relative clause 'who/that'. جوید is a classical present-tense or habitual verb 'seeks/looks for'. نگین 'gem' and کلاه 'hat' are the objects of seeking joined by و 'and'. The clause describes those among the troops who seek gems and hats (i.e., those after material reward).
segments.s210-g209: بپرده درون شد خور تابناک is a compact, somewhat archaic word order: بپرده (into/behind a curtain) is a locative phrase placed before the verb. درون شد literally 'became inside' and functions here as a verbal phrase meaning 'went in/was hidden'. خور تابناک is the noun (خور, 'sun') followed by its adjective تابناک ('radiant'), the normal postnominal adjective order in Persian poetry.
segments.s210-g210: ز introduces a cause or source ('because of' / 'from'), followed by جوش سواران where جوش is a figurative noun 'surge, boiling' describing the energetic movement of سواران ('horsemen'). The phrase و از گرد و خاک links another causal source: از + گرد و خاک = 'out of/from the dust'; گرد here is the noun 'dust' modified by خاک.
segments.s210-g211: ز again marks cause ('because of') and is followed by هرای اسپان where هرای (from هرا) means 'alarm/panic' modifying اسپان ('horses'). و آوای کوس links the second causal element: آوای 'the sound/voice' of کوس ('war-drum'). This is a standard genitive/construct: آوایِ کوس = 'the sound of the drum'.
segments.s210-g212: همی is a frequent classical particle giving a continuous/iterative or emphatic sense ('ever/then'); آسمان 'the sky' is the subject. بر + زمین is the locative direction 'upon the earth'. داد is the past of دادن ('gave'), and بوس is the noun 'a kiss' — together داد بوس = 'gave a kiss' (a poetic personification: the sky 'kissing' the earth).
segments.s210-g213: سپهدار is a title 'commander' followed by the proper name هومان. دِمان (here written دمان) literally means 'hem/skirt' but in such poetic locutions it often denotes the edge/front; پیش صف means 'in front of the battle-line' (پیش = 'before/in front of', صف = 'line/formation'). The whole phrase positions Hōmān at the head of the formation.
segments.s210-g214: یکی is the indefinite numeral 'one' or 'a certain' used here as 'a'; خشت is 'mudbrick' or 'brick' and رخشان is the adjective 'radiant' placed after the noun. گرفته is the perfect participle of گرفتن 'taken/seized', and بکف is an adverbial/locative phrase meaning 'into (his) hand' (ب + کف), so the sequence states that he seized one shining brick into his hand.
segments.s210-g215: همی gives a habitual or emphatic tone 'he kept/then said'. گفت is the verb 'said' (here used as a reporting verb). چون can be 'when' or 'if' depending on the clause; together چون من برایم بجوش functions like 'if/when I can make (things) boil for me' — برایم is 'for me' with the clitic -م attached to برای, and بجوش is a subjunctive/imperative-root form ('let it boil' / 'seethe'), here figuratively 'make them rage/boil' (i.e., arouse violent motion).
segments.s210-g216: برانگیزم is the 1st-person present/subjunctive of برانگیختن 'to rouse/incite' ('I will/let me rouse'). اسپ is 'steed/horse'. و is the coordinating 'and'. برارم here in the manuscript spelling represents برآرم 'I will raise/bring forth' (but the provided lemma list shows a noun; the intended reading is verbal), and خروش is the object 'cry/roar' — 'I will rouse the steed and raise a cry.'
segments.s210-g241: مگر here functions as a conditional 'unless' at the start of the clause. دست is the direct object 'hand' (literally 'take the hand'), گیرد is third-person singular present (used as a jussive/indicative in classical poetry) meaning 'takes' or 'seizes', and جهاندار ما is the subject phrase 'our world-ruler/sovereign' (جهاندار modified by the pronoun ما). Word order is somewhat inverted (verb before subject), a common poetic flexibility.
segments.s210-g242: وگر نه is a coordinating conditional 'and if not / otherwise'. بد است is the predication 'is bad' (بد = adjective, است = copula). اختر کار ما is a noun phrase 'the star of our affair' (اختر = 'star', کار ما = 'our affair/fate') used metaphorically for 'our fortune'; the whole clause says 'otherwise the star of our affairs is bad.'
segments.s210-g243: کنون is an adverb 'now' introducing the command. نامداران is a vocative/plural noun 'renowned ones' or 'noble men.' زرینه کفش is an adjectival phrase 'golden shoes' (زرینه premodifies کفش). The phrase sets up what follows as the object/attire for the addressed men.
segments.s210-g244: بباشید is the second-person plural imperative of بودن 'to be' used stylistically to mean 'be (clad/arrayed)' or 'stand' (i.e., 'put on' / 'be adorned with'). با is the preposition 'with' linking to کاویانی درفش, where کاویانی qualifies درفش as 'Kavian' (the legendary/Kayanian) and درفش means 'banner/standard.' Together: an imperative to be in golden shoes and to be with the Kayanian banner.
segments.s210-g245: ازین is the prepositional phrase 'from this' (از + این with poetic spelling), کوه‌پایه is the noun 'foothill.' مجنبید is the negative imperative (second-person plural) of جنبیدن 'do not move / do not stir.' هیچ follows and reinforces the negation ('at all' / 'in no way'). The whole unit commands staying put: 'Do not move at all from this foothill.'
segments.s210-g246: نه is a negative particle here negating the clause 'روز نبرد است' = 'it is a day of battle' (روز = 'day', نبرد = 'battle', است = copula). Thus 'it is not a day of battle.' و introduces the following phrase گاه بسیچ, where گاه = 'time/period' and بسیچ = 'much/many,' i.e. 'and there is plenty of time' (meaning there is no immediate need to fight).
segments.s210-g247: همانا که is a discourse opener 'verily/indeed that' introducing a declarative remark. از ما بهر یک (literally 'from us for one') expresses a ratio 'for each one of us' (از ما = 'of us', بهر = 'for' or 'for the benefit of / per'). The following numerals (یک دویست) specify the count 'one hundred and two hundred' in the line meaning 'two hundred for each one.'
segments.s210-g248: فزونست is a verbal/adjectival predicate 'is more / there are more.' بدخواه is the plural noun 'ill-wishers / enemies' serving as the subject. اگر بیش نیست is a qualifying clause 'if there is not more' (اگر = 'if', بیش = 'more', نیست = 'is not'), so the full meaning: 'ill-wishers are numerous (two hundred for each of us), if not more.'
segments.s210-g185: بلشکر functions as the speaker/topic (‘the army/army‑chief’ in classical usage) followed by the adverb چنین + گفت ‘thus said’. The pattern is Subject — manner — verb, and the following هومان شیر is the reported predicate (direct speech) introduced without quotation marks, a common poetic device.
segments.s210-g186: که‌ای is a relative/introducing particle here meaning ‘who / that is’. رزم دیده is a compound adjective: رزم (battle) + دیده (past participle ‘seen’) producing ‘battle‑experienced’. سران دلیر is a nominal phrase 'foremost/bravest (among) the heads/ chiefs' with سران as a superlative/collective notion and دلیر as the qualifying adjective; together they modify the antecedent هومان.
segments.s210-g187: چو here means 'when' (a temporal subordinate). روشن شود is a light verb construction where شود (become) gives an inchoative sense 'becomes bright'. تیره شب is the noun phrase 'dark night' (adjective before noun), and روز ماست is the main clause 'it is our day' with the copula ست at the end — typical Persian SOV/Predicate‑final order.
segments.s210-g188: که introduces a clause of explanation 'for/that'. این اختر گیتی‌افروز is a nominal phrase 'this star, world‑illuminating' (گیتی‑افروز is a compound adjective meaning 'illuminating the world'), and ماست is the copula 'is ours' — again the predicate copula appears sentence‑finally.
segments.s210-g189: شما را uses را in a beneficiary/dative sense 'for you'; همه = 'all/entirely' emphasizing shadkāmi; شادکامی = 'happiness/joy'; بود is a literary/polite optative here (a common formula in epic lines) rendering the clause as a wish: 'May you all have happiness.'
segments.s210-g190: مرا = 'for me' (beneficiary/dative); خوبی و نیکنامی = 'goodness and good repute (a good name)'; بود again expresses a wish/blessing in this formulaic context: 'May there be goodness and a good name for me.' (Note: the surface token 'م' in the word list corresponds to مرا here as a contracted/poetic form.)
segments.s210-g191: ز is the preposition 'from' placed before its noun (poetic register). لشکر is the noun 'army' (object of ز). همی here functions as the archaic/progressive particle often used with past narration ('kept/was ...ing' or emphatic 'did'), and برخروشید is the past tense verb 'rose up/roared'; طوس, the proper name, comes after the verb (poetic word order: verb + subject).
segments.s210-g192: شب تیره is a noun + adjective (night + dark), adjective after the noun as usual in Persian poetry. تا is the subordinating preposition 'until' taking the time-word گاه ('time') as its complement; بانگ خروس is a genitive-like juxtaposition 'the cry of the rooster' giving the terminating time.
segments.s210-g65: تو is the nominative pronoun 'you'. با is the preposition 'with' introducing the following nouns. خوی + ش here form a possessive phrase (خویش), the enclitic ش = 'his' attached to خوی (kin/kinship), so together 'his kinsmen' or 'his kin'; the orthography here shows them split. وپیوند is 'و پیوند' (the conjunction fused to the noun) meaning 'and alliance/bond'. و connects that compound to چندین سوار ('several horsemen'); چندین modifies سوار. The word order is preposition + noun-phrases joined by و, yielding a coordinated accompaniment of 'you'.
segments.s210-g66: همه پهلوان is a noun phrase 'all (the) champions' where همه ('all/everyone') precedes the noun پهلوان. The following و همه نامدار repeats the distributive/emphatic pattern: 'and all (the) famed (ones)'; نامدار functions as an adjective used as a substantive 'famous/renowned ones.' The repetition of همه is emphatic — 'everyone, all of them.'
segments.s210-g67: بخیره here functions adverbially ('to/into good fortune' or 'to safety/fortune') and is attached to the negative imperative مده ('do not give' from دادن) — so 'do not give (yourself) to fortune/safety.' خویشتن را is the direct object 'yourself' marked by را. بباد is a fixed expression meaning 'to the wind' / 'be blown away' or 'be lost'; with خویشتن را it means 'do not let yourself be blown away/be lost.'
segments.s210-g68: بباید is a modal verb 'must/ought' that introduces the subordinate clause with که ('that'). پند من is the noun phrase 'my counsel/advice' where من is the possessor. آیدت is the verb آید ('comes') plus the enclitic ت = 'to you' (third-person verb with a second-person clitic object), functioning like a present/subjunctive 'may/should come to you' or 'comes to you.' یاد here means 'remembrance' — 'that my advice comes to your remembrance' = 'that you remember my counsel.'
segments.s210-g69: سزاوار is an adjective 'fit/appropriate' and is followed by the verbal noun کشتن ('killing'), giving an impersonal predication 'fit to kill.' هرآنکس که هست is a relative clause 'whoever there is' or 'anyone who exists/presents himself' formed with هرآنکس + relative که + copula هست. The clause as a whole means 'it is fitting to kill whoever there is.'
segments.s210-g70: بمان is the imperative 'remain/stay.' تا introduces a temporal clause 'until.' بیازند is a third-person plural verb (subjunctive/optative force) from یازیدن meaning 'open/burst forth' — here it implies 'they open/attack' or 'they break forth.' بر is the preposition 'upon/against' governing کینه 'grudge/hatred.' دست ('hand') appears after the prepositional phrase as in the idiom 'دست بر کینه' meaning to set hands upon the grudge (i.e., to take action/revenge). So the clause reads 'stay until they strike out against the grudge.'
segments.s210-g71: کزین is a prepositional compound 'from this' (کِ + زِ + این), introducing the source 'from this grudge.' کینه is the noun 'grudge/hatred.' مرد گنهکار is the noun phrase 'a sinful man' (مرد 'man' modified by the adjective گنهکار 'sinner'). هیچ is the indefinite/negative 'none/no' emphasizing that there is no escape. Word order places the source phrase first for emphasis.
segments.s210-g72: رهایی نیابد is a negated verbal phrase: رهایی 'release/freedom' as direct object followed by نیابد (from یافتن) 'does not attain/find' (third-person). خرد را is the noun 'wisdom/intellect' with the object marker را, and مپیچ is the negative imperative 'do not twist/turn/evade' (from پیچیدن) addressed to the listener: 'do not baffle/mislead the intellect.' The order places the descriptive clause first and then an imperative admonition.
segments.s210-g25: This clause uses an older/poetic verb form with the b- prefix: بجنگ here functions as a past/perfective occurrence 'fought' (verb first, as in classical word order), followed by the object/participant. من is the first‑person pronoun 'me/I' as the object of بجنگ, ارژنگ is the proper name (subject of the verb), and روز نبرد is a time phrase 'on the day of battle' placed after the verb phrase. The order Verb – Object – Subject – Time is typical in Ferdowsi's elevated diction.
segments.s210-g26: کجا 'where' opens a question about location or state. داشتی is the past/imperfect form of داشتن ('you had/you were keeping'), so خویشتن را (literally 'yourself' with را as an emphatic/accusative marker) is the internal object: 'where had you kept yourself'. بمرد is an optative/subjunctive/past‑oriented clause meaning 'that (you) died' or 'were as if dead' — here it functions as the speaker's rhetorical suggestion ('had you died?').
segments.s210-g27: دلیران (plural) 'the brave' is the subject; لشکر is in apposition or a genitival/noun modifier ('of the host/army'), producing 'the brave of the host'. ندارند is the 3rd person plural of داشتن with negation implied by context here (in classical lines it functions as 'do not have'): 'do not have shame'. شرم is the abstract noun 'shame' as direct object. Word order Subject–Modifier–Verb–Object is preserved.
segments.s210-g28: نجوشد is a classical/poetic form of جوشیدن with the negative prefix ن-: here it carries an optative/desired sense 'may not... cease to boil' or 'let not ... stop seething' — i.e. an exhortation that passion continue. یکی را uses را as an object marker/emphatic ('not even one'); برگ literally 'leaf' is used metaphorically (a poetical image for a drop or small piece), خون 'blood', and گرم here means 'hot/fervent' (emotional heat). The clause therefore says, figuratively, that not one warm leaf/drop of blood should stop seething.
segments.s210-g29: که introduces a result/reason clause ('for' / 'that'). پیکار ایشان is 'their combat' (پیکار = battle/fight, ایشان = their). سپهبد is the title 'spahbed' (general) and کند is the 3rd person singular of کردن 'does/makes' used here to mean 'would cause/compel' in a classical/resultive sense. Put together: 'for their fighting would drive/compel even the commander.'
segments.s210-g30: ایندرون 'inward/inner' (here 'in the inner field' or 'within the battle'); برزم is an archaic verbal/nominal form related to ریزیدن/رزیدن meaning 'to tremble/fall' or here more generally 'the battlefield' or 'battle‑scene' (classical usage is compressed). دستشان 'their hands' (their strength/action), بد 'ill/bad/disastrous', and کند 'does/makes'. Grammatically this clause states the result of their action: 'at the heart of the fight their hands do harm' i.e. 'their hands wreak havoc in the inner battle.'
segments.s210-g31: کجا 'where' poses the lamenting question. The names بیژن and گیو are listed in conjunction (و = 'and'). آزادگان is a plural substantive 'the free/noble ones' describing those heroes. Word order is interrogative with the interrogative adverb first followed by the enumerated names and their descriptive epithet.
segments.s210-g32: This is a continuation of the catalogue of heroes: جهانگیر (a personal name/title), گودرز (Goudarz, a proper name), and کشوادگان (a collective/epithet, an archaic/poetic descriptor of a group of noble/valiant men). The line compresses names and epithets without an explicit verb, typical of rhetorical lists in Ferdowsi.
segments.s210-g73: This is a compact nominal + verb clause in the order object–subject–adverb–verb–object: 'مرا' (me / to me) + 'شاه ایران' (the King of Iran) + 'چنین' (thus/this) + 'داد' (gave) + 'پند' (advice). In classical Persian the clitic object marker 'را' is written separately here, so 'مرا' functions as the indirect/accusative object 'me' ("gave me"). 'داد' is a simple past of دادن (gave).
segments.s210-g74: 'که' introduces a reported content clause ("that..."). 'پیران' is the plural subject "elders." 'نباید' is an impersonal negative modal meaning "should not / must not." The second 'که' again introduces the subordinate verb phrase; 'یابد' is 3rd‑person singular of یافتن used here with a subordinate sense ("to find / suffer") — semantically 'to incur' — and in tone it has a subjunctive/optative flavor following نباید. 'گزند' is the abstract object "harm."
segments.s210-g75: Again 'که' introduces a clause giving further information: 'that he ...'. 'او' = 'he.' 'ویژه' here means 'especially / set apart' (appointed). 'پروردگار' is a title-like noun 'lord / protector.' 'منست' is written as a single word (classical contraction of 'من است' or an adjectival copula); in plain modern Persian you'd expect 'من است' meaning 'is mine' or 'is for me' — here it functions as the copula linking 'ویژه پروردگار' to the speaker.
segments.s210-g76: This is a continuation describing the same person with two coordinated predicates: 'جهاندیده' (worldly‑wise, experienced) and 'دوستدار' (affectionate/attached to). The final 'منست' again functions as the copula/predicate-marker: 'he is worldly‑wise and is fond of me.' The adjectives/nouns precede the copula as is normal in Persian.
segments.s210-g77: 'به بیداد بر خیره با او مکوش' is an admonition built with negative imperative 'مکوش' (do not strive/endeavour). 'به بیداد' literally 'with injustice' or 'in injustice'; 'بر' here marks the target/againstness ('against'); 'خیره' describes those who are stunned/bewildered (an adjective used substantively). 'با او' ('with him') indicates the person one might contend with. The whole phrase: 'Do not, by injustice, contend against the bewildered with him.'
segments.s210-g78: 'نگه کن' is an imperative compound meaning 'beware / keep watch / pay heed' (نگه = keep/watch; کن = do! imperative). 'که' introduces the reason/explanation clause that follows. 'دارد' here acts as an auxiliary or present-tense 'has/is about to' (literally 'he has'); it introduces what he possesses or is going to do. 'بپند' in context refers to admonition/counsel 'for giving advice to' (the lexical list given does not show that sense exactly); 'تو گوش' ('to your ear') is a dative/locative phrase indicating the counsel is directed to you. So: 'Be on your guard, for he has counsel for your ear.'
segments.s210-g79: 'چنین گفت هومان' is 'Thus Homan spoke' (چنین = thus; گفت here functions as the past-tense verb 'said/spoke' attached to the name 'هومان'). 'به بیداد و داد' lists the topics 'on injustice and (on) justice' with the preposition 'به' marking the theme/subject of speaking. Word order is typical: adverbial/marker + verb + subject, then prepositional phrase giving what was spoken about.
segments.s210-g80: 'چو' here = 'when.' 'فرمان دهد' is a temporal subordinate clause: 'when (he) gave the command' — classical narrative often uses the present/subjunctive-form 'دهد' to narrate a past action. 'شاه فرخ نژاد' is the subject 'King Farrokh‑nizad' (an epithet + lineage noun acting as name). The clause places Homan's speech at the moment the king issued his command.
segments.s210-g225: ابا here is a verb meaning 'to be reluctant/grudge' used with a prohibitive construction; گنج دینار are the direct objects 'treasure and dinars' (noun phrase). جفتی مکن is a negative imperative ('do not ... in pairs' literally) so the whole phrase idiomatically means 'do not begrudge/withhold treasure and coin.' Word order is poetic (verb/adjective first, objects following).
segments.s210-g226: ز بهر is a prepositional phrase 'for/since for the sake of'; سلیح is 'arms, weapons'. ایچ is an intensifying negative adverb 'not at all', and زفتی مکن is a prohibitive clause 'do not be mean/nasty' (مکن negates زفتی). Together: 'for the sake of arms, do not at all be stingy.'
segments.s210-g227: که introduces a purpose/optative clause 'that / so that'; امروز 'today'; گردیم is a present-subjunctive/optative form 'may we become' (poetic verb form), and پیروزگر is the predicate/adjective 'victorious' or 'triumphant'. The clause expresses a wish/result: 'that today we may become triumphant.'
segments.s210-g228: بیابد is subjunctive/optative 'may (one) find/attain' (here used as a wish); دل is the thing affected 'the heart'; از + اختر نیک is a prepositional phrase 'from the auspicious/fortunate star'; بر is a locative/relative particle 'upon/above'. The sequence means 'may the heart find/obtain something from the auspicious star above.'
segments.s210-g229: وزین is an adjective 'dignified, weighty' modifying روی 'face/mien'; لشکر سپهدار طوس is a genitive chain 'the army of the commander Tūs' (لشکر = army, سپهدار = commander/title, طوس = Tūs). The whole noun phrase describes the host's stately appearance.
segments.s210-g230: بیاراست is a past/perfective or perfective-stem 'adorned/arrayed' (he/it adorned); برسان is a causative/imperative-type or present-stem 'bring/caused to reach' (here 'caused to reach' or 'brought about'); چشم functions as 'sight' or 'what is seen', and خروش is 'a cry/roar'. Together the clause says that the arraying produced a sight that drew/let loose a cry.
segments.s210-g231: بروبر is an adverb 'face-to-face / right before'; یلان is the plural 'heroes'; آفرین is an interjection/acclamation 'bravo!' and خواندند is a past-tense verb 'they uttered/declared/called'. The sequence means 'right before the heroes they shouted “Bravo!”'.
segments.s210-g232: ورا is an indirect-object/locative pronoun 'to/at him'; پهلوان زمین is a title phrase 'champion (pahlavān) of the earth' (زمین = the earth/world); خواندند again is past 'they called/summoned'. Grammatically: indirect object + title + verb = 'they called him the champion of the world.'
segments.s210-g153: بران is an adverbial particle here meaning 'thereupon/upon that' and sets the temporal/contiguity frame for what follows. نامور is the noun 'the famed/renowned (man)' and functions as the affected person. تیرباران is a compound noun 'a shower of arrows' and گرفت is used in a semi‑passive/intransitive sense ('was seized/struck by' or 'was smitten by'), so the whole clause says that the famed man was overtaken by a rain of arrows.
segments.s210-g154: چپ و راست is an adverbial phrase 'on the left and right' (i.e., on both sides). جنگ is the agent/subject 'battle' and سواران is the patient 'the horsemen'; گرفت is here active 'seized/took' (battle took/seized the horsemen). The line therefore states that, on both sides, the horsemen were taken up by the fighting.
segments.s210-g155: ز introduces material or source ('of/from'), so the list that follows names the materials: پولاد 'steel', پیکان 'arrow(s)', then و 'and' links پر 'feather/plume' with عقاب 'eagle' ('an eagle's plume'). This is a compact material-phrase: 'of steel, arrows and an eagle’s plume'.
segments.s210-g156: سپر is the direct object 'shield' and کرد (light verb 'to do/make') is the main verb meaning 'made' or 'put up' — together 'he made/held up a shield'. بر پیش روی آفتاب is a prepositional phrase: بر 'on/against/before', پیش 'in front of/before', روی 'face' and آفتاب 'the sun' — literally 'before the sun's face', i.e. he held the shield up facing the sun.
segments.s210-g157: چون here is temporal 'when/as'. ز شب رفته is a perfect participial phrase 'night having gone' (i.e. dawn). دو پاس گشت literally 'turned two-pas' — گشت is the intransitive verb 'became/turned', and دو پاس functions adverbially 'to both sides' or 'in two directions'; overall: 'when night had departed, the world turned both ways.'
segments.s210-g158: همه 'all/every' modifies روی 'face/surface' so همه روی کشور means 'every face/surface of the country'. پر is the adjective/postposition 'full (of)' and الماس is the filler 'diamonds'; گشت is 'became'. Together: 'every face of the realm became full of diamonds' (a poetic way to say the land glittered with jewels/sunlight).
segments.s210-g159: ز introduces cause/agent 'by/with'. تیر خدنگ is a compound noun 'an arrow (tipped like) a dagger' or 'a dagger‑like arrow' (خدنگ qualifying تیر). اسپ هومان 'Homan's steed' is the affected noun. بخست is an archaic/poetic past form here indicating the sudden affliction of the horse (i.e. it was struck/felled); in classical usage such verbs can denote being hit or collapsing, so the clause means 'Homan's horse was struck/overthrown by a dagger‑arrow.'
segments.s210-g160: تن 'body' is the theme; بارگی is used metaphorically (literally 'foliage' or 'bearing') to describe the body’s state—here implying the body fell or hung lifeless. گشت 'became' links the metaphor to the result. با 'with/by' + خاک پست 'low/dusty earth' indicates the place or medium ('to the dusty ground'). Together: 'his body became (as) foliage/was laid with the low dust' — idiomatically: 'his body fell to the dusty ground.'
segments.s210-g239: همه دست یکسر بیزدان زنیم is a single clause with theme-before-verb word order typical of epic poetry. همه دست (literally “all hands”) is the subject phrase, یکسر is an adverb meaning “entirely/at once” modifying the action, بیزدان (the object) = “the godless/impious,” placed before the verb for emphasis, and زنیم is a first-person-plural verb form (–یم) used here in a jussive/optative sense (“let us strike” / “we should strike”).
segments.s210-g240: منی از تن خویش بفگنیم is a short clause where منی is a poetic/emphatic form built from من + the enclitic ی (used here to mark an emphatic/nominalized ‘the me’ or ‘myself’); از تن خویش is a prepositional phrase “from (our) body,” where تن = “body” and خویش is the reflexive/possessive formed from خوی + ش = “one's own.” بفگنیم is an archaic/poetic first-person-plural subjunctive/jussive (ب- + stem + -یم) meaning roughly “let us rend/separate/cast away” (root fgn/fgn- is rare; sense is inferred from context). The clause therefore expresses a wish/command: “let us sever the self from our body.”
segments.s210-g81: بران رفت باید ببیچارگی is a compressed poetic clause: بران ('from/away from that') + رفت ('go/leave') + باید ('must/it is necessary') + ببیچارگی ('helplessness, wretchedness'). Word order is inverted for effect: the predicate 'must go' (رفت باید) refers back to ببیچارگی, so the sense is 'that wretchedness must be sent away.'
segments.s210-g82: سپردن بدو دل بیکبارگی is an infinitival phrase that completes the thought: سپردن ('to entrust/hand over') + بدو ('to him') + دل ('the heart') + بیکبارگی ('suddenness, abruptly'). The infinitive governs the object order (سپردن دل بدو), here stylistically reversed; the final adverbial بیکبارگی qualifies the manner ('suddenly/abruptly').
segments.s210-g83: همان رزم پیران نه بر آرزوست: همان ('that, the very') modifies رزم ('battle'); پیران ('of the elders') is a genitival/noun modifier. نه + بر + آرزوست negates the predicate 'is (for) desire' — literally 'is not upon desire' meaning 'is not motivated by wishful longing.'
segments.s210-g84: که او راد و آزاده و نیک خوست is a relative/explanatory clause introduced by که ('for/that'), giving the reason: او ('he') + a series of predicates joined by و ('and') — راد ('generous'), آزاده ('free/noble‑hearted'), نیک خوست (a stock expression meaning 'of good disposition' or 'well‑wishing'). The copular sense ('is') is implied; the adjectives describe his character.
segments.s210-g85: بدین گفت و گوی اندرون بود طوس: بدین ('by/with this' — here marking 'in/at this [exchange]') + گفت و گوی ('conversation/exchange') + اندرون بود ('was inside') + طوس (the subject, Tūs). Word order places the locative/scene (بدین گفت و گوی) before the verb 'was' and the name.
segments.s210-g86: که شد گیو را روی چون سندروس: که ('so/that') introduces the result clause; شد ('became') is the verb; گیو را ('Giv' with the object/ topical marker را) is the person whose face changed; روی ('face') is the affected noun; چون ('like') + سندروس ('sandarūs, fragrant wood') forms a simile: 'his face became like sandalwood' (i.e., rosy/fragrant appearance).
segments.s210-g87: ز لشکر بیامد بکردار باد is an optative statement: ز لشکر ('from the army/host') + بیامد ('may come' / 'come') + بکردار ('of good conduct, virtuous') + باد (an optative particle 'may it be'). Together this reads as a wish or announcement: 'May one of good conduct come from the host.'
segments.s210-g88: چنین گفت کای طوس فرخ نژاد: چنین گفت ('thus he said') introduces the quotation; کای is a vocative 'O' addressing طوس (Tūs); فرخ نژاد ('fortunate-born', 'of auspicious lineage') follows as an epithet applied to Tūs. This is a direct address: 'O Tūs, fortunate of lineage.'
segments.s210-g193: همی گفت is a narrative/perfective construction meaning 'kept saying' or 'said repeatedly'; هومان is the speaker's name following the verb (poetic order). چه مرد منست is an exclamatory nominal clause ('what a man he is'); منست here functions as a predicative element (older/poetic form attached to the adjective/nominal phrase) rather than a modern copula.
segments.s210-g194: که introduces a relative clause modifying 'مرد' ('who/whom'); پیل is 'elephant' and ژیان here qualifies that noun in a fixed phrase (poetic compound referring to war/strong elephants). هم‌نبرد is used as 'not matched / rival in battle' and functions as the predicate inside the relative clause; منست again is the poetic predicative marker completing the clause.
segments.s210-g195: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'as/when'. چرخ بلند is 'the high/lofty sky' (noun + adjective). از شبه تاج کرد literally 'made a crown out of the resemblance' — از marks source/material, شبه is 'likeness', تاج is the direct object 'crown', and کرد is the past tense verb 'made'. Word order (subject — oblique phrase — object — verb) reflects poetic compression.
segments.s210-g196: شمامه ('fragrance' or 'scent') is the agent/subject and پراگند is the verbal/adjectival form meaning 'scattered/was scattered' (here functioning as the action 'scattered'). بر لاژورد is the prepositional phrase 'upon the lapis‑lazuli' showing the location/target of the scattering.
segments.s210-g197: طلایه is the subject 'the vanguard'. ز هر سو is a prepositional phrase 'from every side' (ز + هر + سو). برون is the adverb 'forth/out'. تاختند is the 3rd person plural past tense 'they charged/rushed'; subject–adverb–verb order is typical and the verb's plural ending agrees with طلایه (treated collectively).
segments.s210-g198: بهر is the preposition 'for/toward' indicating purpose or direction; پرده‌ای is an indefinite noun 'a curtain/screen' (the -ای ending here gives indefiniteness). پاسبان is the direct object 'guards' and ساختند is the past plural 'they made/placed' — together 'they set up guards for a curtain'.
segments.s210-g223: بپیران is the prepositional phrase 'بِ + پیران' (poetic 'to the elders'), placed before the verb. چنین گفت is a fixed word order meaning 'said thus/so' (چنین 'thus' + گفت 'said'). کای is an archaic vocative marker 'O', directly addressing the addressee; پهلوان is the vocative noun 'champion/hero'.
segments.s210-g224: تو is the emphatic 2nd-person subject 'you' (often optional in Persian but here retained for force). بگشای is an imperative/optative form 'open/unloose' (classical form with prefix بـ). بند سلیح is the object 'the strap/fetter of the weapon' (نouns in construct without an explicit ezāfe in poetry). گوان is a postpositive adverbial/qualifier 'like/so', modifying the preceding phrase.
segments.s210-g233: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause 'who/for that'. پیروزگر بود is a nominal predicate with بود as the past tense copula 'was' (پیروزگر 'victor/triumphant'). روز نبرد is a temporal noun phrase 'the day of battle' (روز + noun in genitive relation).
segments.s210-g234: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. هومان and ویسه are proper names (Houmān and Viseh); word order here places the source (ز هومان) before the patient (ویسه). برآورد گرد is a two-part classical verbal phrase: برآورد ('brought/caused to bring forth') plus گرد, an older auxiliary/verbal particle used in compound tenses or to make the action past/passive in narrative style.
segments.s210-g235: سپهبد is the title 'spahbed/commander'. بگودرز is a dative expression 'to Goudarz' (بـ as the dative marker). کشواد is the name of the addressee (Keshvad) or the person being spoken with; گفت is the past tense verb 'said'. The sequence is title + recipient + name + verb, common in epic narration.
segments.s210-g236: که introduces reported speech or a content clause 'that'. این راز 'this secret' is the direct object/topic of the clause. بر کس is the idiom 'from/for anyone' (بر used in the sense 'to/for' with کس 'anyone'). نباید is a modal auxiliary 'must not / should not', and نهفت is the present-subjunctive/infinitive form of نهفتن 'to hide' (here meaning 'be hidden'); together نباید نهفت = 'should not be hidden'.
segments.s210-g237: اگر introduces a conditional 'if'. لشکر ما is the subject noun phrase 'our army' (لشکر + possessive ما). پذیره is a predicate/adjective meaning 'received/accepted' (in classical usage it can mean 'welcomed' or 'given shelter'). شوند is the 3rd-person plural subjunctive/passive 'become/are' (here marking the conditional protasis: 'if our army are received').
segments.s210-g238: سواران is the subject 'horsemen/riders'. بدخواه is an adjective 'ill-wishing/hostile' modifying سواران. چیره is a predicate meaning 'dominant/prevailing' (here adjectival/verb-like), and شوند (again 3rd-person plural subjunctive) completes the verb phrase 'will become prevailing' — together the apodosis: 'the hostile horsemen will prevail.' Note adjective-after-noun order typical of Persian.
segments.s210-g49: که here introduces a subordinate/relative clause ('that' / 'who'). پر درد باشم is a predicative phrase: پر + noun = 'full of (noun)'; باشم is first‑person singular present subjunctive/optative of بودن used to express a wish or potential state ('may I be / that I be'). ز is the poetic preposition 'because of / on account of', and مردان مرد is a fixed emphatic compound 'men of men' = 'the truly brave/heroic men'.
segments.s210-g50: که begins this relative clause modifying 'مردان مرد' ('who'). پیش من is a prepositional phrase 'in my presence / before me' (preposition before its object). آیند is third‑person plural present/subjunctive 'they come' (here relative/optative sense 'who come'), and روز نبرد is a temporal noun phrase 'the day of battle' placed after the verb for poetic word order.
segments.s210-g51: پس از is the prepositional combination 'after'; it introduces a list of proper names (رستم زال سام) that function as temporal/benchmark references. سوار ('rider/horseman') is the head noun of the phrase that will be completed by the following verb (its object/contrast 'a rider (like you)' is what the speaker has or has not seen).
segments.s210-g52: ندیدم is the simple past first‑person negative 'I did not see'. چو is the comparative particle 'like/as', linking the following phrase as the standard of comparison. تو is 'you'; نیز adds 'also/likewise' or emphasizes the comparison; یک نامدار 'a single renowned one' is an indefinite nominal group meaning 'a single famed person/rider'. The whole string is the object of ندیدم: 'I have not seen a renowned one like you.'
segments.s210-g53: پدر بر پدر is a poetic reduplication meaning 'father upon father' or 'ancestral line / generations'. نامبردار is an adjective 'renowned' describing these fathers. و شاه adds 'and king(s)' — this is a compact listing of illustrious ancestors and rulers; word order and repetition are stylistic and typical of epic poetry.
segments.s210-g54: چو here introduces a conditional/temporal clause ('when/if'). تو جنگجویی is an appositive noun phrase 'you, a warrior' (جنگجویی = 'a warrior' or 'warlike one'). نیاید is third‑person negative present/subjunctive 'does not come / is not produced', and سپاه ('army') is the subject placed after the verb for poetic inversion — together: 'if the army does not produce/bring a warrior like you.'
segments.s210-g55: تو شو is a second‑person imperative 'you, become / stand up'. تا introduces purpose/result here ('so that / in order that'). ز لشکر is 'from the host/army' and یکی نامجوی is 'a certain name‑seeker' (نامجوی = 'one who seeks a name/reputation', i.e. a challenger seeking fame). The clause expresses the purpose of the imperative: 'stand up so that a challenger may come from the army.'
segments.s210-g56: بیاید is an optative/jussive third‑person form 'may he come / let him come'. بروی is second‑person singular subjunctive/imperative 'you go / advance'. اندر is the preposition 'in / among / into'. آریم is a poetic first‑person plural form from the root آرد/آوردن meaning 'we bring/put' (here 'we will bring into confrontation'), and روی is the noun 'face' — together, 'let him come; you advance and we will bring (the two) face to face.'
segments.s210-g9: بهومان is a prepositional/Directional phrase introducing to whom the speech is addressed; چنین is an adverb 'thus/so' modifying گفت; گفت here functions as the past-tense verb 'he said'; کای is the vocative particle (کَ + ای) 'O' addressing شوربخت, which is a direct vocative 'wretched/unfortunate one.' Word order is [direction/state] + [manner] + گفت + vocative.
segments.s210-g10: ز introduces origin/source 'from'; پالیز is the noun 'orchard/field'; کین is the abstract noun 'hatred/vengeance' modifying or forming a genitive with درخت; برنیامد is the negative past compound verb 'did not arise/appear' (بر‑+ نیامد); درخت is the subject 'tree' (here figurative: 'a tree of hatred'). The clause reads literally 'from the orchard a tree of hatred did not come forth.'
segments.s210-g11: نمودم is first-person singular past 'I showed / I made appear'; بارژنگ is a compound noun 'clangor/sound of war' functioning as the direct object; یک دست برد is a nominal phrase 'a one‑handed victory' (یک‌دست modifies برد). Here the verb نمودم governs the following noun phrase describing what was shown or produced.
segments.s210-g12: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause 'who/which'; بود is the past copula 'was'; از شما marks membership/association 'of/among you'; نامبردار is an adjective/noun 'renowned, notable'; و is the conjunction 'and'; گرد here is an epithet/old adjective meaning roughly 'famed/celebrated' or 'around/among' in older usage. Together the clause qualifies the preceding victory or person: 'who was renowned among you and celebrated.'
segments.s210-g13: تو is the 2nd‑person subject 'you'; اکنون 'now' and همانا 'indeed/verily' are temporal/emphatic adverbs; بکین is the infinitival/verbial phrase meaning 'to take vengeance' (a poetic variant) and آمدی is the 2nd‑person past 'you came.' The sequence places the purpose/intent (بکین) before the main verb آمدی: 'you have come for vengeance.'
segments.s210-g14: که here introduces an explanatory clause 'for/that' giving the circumstance of آمدی; با is the preposition 'with/by'; خشت is 'mudbrick'; بر is the preposition 'on/upon'; پشت is 'back'; زین is 'saddle'; آمدی repeats the verb 'you came.' Grammatically this is a circumstantial clause: 'for you came with a mudbrick on the saddle's back.'
segments.s210-g15: بجان is an oath formula 'by the life of'; و is the coordinating conjunction 'and'; سر is 'head' (here paired with 'life' as an oathful phrase); شاه ایران is the noun phrase 'the Shah/king of Iran'; سپاه is the noun 'army/host.' The phrase functions as an oath or emphatic preface: 'By the life and head of the King of Iran, [the] army ...'.
segments.s210-g16: که introduces a relative clause describing سپاه: بی‌جوشن is 'without cuirass/armor' (بی‑ = without + جوشن cuirass); و links nouns/phrases; گرز is 'mace' (weapon); و; رومی is the adjective 'Roman/Byzantine'; کلاه is 'helmet' (here 'Roman helmet'). Syntactically the clause lists the army's equipment/condition: 'which was without cuirass and (possessed) maces and Roman helmets.'
segments.s210-g171: This noun phrase names the participants: همه (an inclusive quantifier) + نامداران (plural noun 'renowned ones') modified by the adjective/epithet پرخاشجوی ('quarrelsome' or 'aggressive'). Word order is determiner/quantifier → head noun → adjectival modifier, typical for Persian compounds in poetry.
segments.s210-g172: یکایک is an adverb 'one by one / each in turn'; بدو is the prepositional pronoun 'to him/unto him'; در نهادند is the past tense 3rd‑pl. of نهادن 'to place/put' used here in the idiom روی در نهادن meaning 'to turn (one's) face toward'; روی is the object 'face'. The implied subject 'they' refers back to the NP in the previous segment.
segments.s210-g173: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'; شد is the past 'became'; روز تاریک 'the day dark' is the noun + adjective; و links to the following phrase; بیگاه here is an adverb 'untimely / suddenly' modifying the following verb; گشت is another past verb 'turned / became'. The double verb (شد ... گشت) is a poetic amplification: both verbs mark the change of time/condition.
segments.s210-g174: ز here is the preposition 'from / because of'; جنگ یلان is a genitival phrase 'the battle (of) the heroes' or 'the heroes' fighting'; دست کوتاه گشت is an idiom: دست (literally 'hand') used as 'power/ability', کوتاه 'short' and گشت 'became' → 'their power was curtailed' or 'they lacked strength.' The subject is the same heroes referenced before.
segments.s210-g175: بپیچید is a past form of پیچیدن with the prefix ب‑ used here as 'turned' (he turned); هومان is the proper name 'Houman' (the actor of the verb); جنگی qualifies عنان, giving 'war/ battle‑reins' (an adjectival use). The verb precedes the name due to poetic word order but functions as a simple past narrative.
segments.s210-g176: سپهبد is the subject 'the spahbed / general'; بدو 'to him' (i.e. toward that person); راست کرده is a compound participial construction literally 'having made straight' (راست = straight, کرده = made/done) modifying the following noun سنان 'spear' — together meaning 'aimed his spear straight at him' (کرده as perfect participle). Word order places the indirect object (بدو) before the participial phrase.
segments.s210-g177: بنزدیک is an adverb/adpositional phrase 'near / close at hand'; پیران here functions as a proper name (Piran) but the supplied lemma senses list did not include the proper‑name sense (see senses mapping); شد is 'became / came' in past tense; از رزمگاه 'from the battlefield' gives the origin or direction of the approach. The implied subject (he/one) is carried over from the narrative.
segments.s210-g178: خروشی is a noun 'a roar / cry'; برآمد is the past verb 'arose / issued forth'; ز توران سپاه is a prepositional phrase 'from the Turanian army/host' with ز before the genitive construction (توران = Turan, سپاه = army). This is a simple subject‑verb‑adverbial phrase describing a shout from the host.
segments.s210-g57: بدو گفت = 'to him said' — بدو is the prepositional clitic phrase 'to him/unto him' placed before the verb, and گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' (classical word order places the indirect object before the verb). طوس‌ای is the vocative form addressing Tūs with the suffix -ای, and سرافراز مرد is an epithet 'proud/honored man' used after the vocative.
segments.s210-g58: This is a nominal predicate clause where سپهبد (‘spahbed’, a high military title) precedes the copula idea; منم = 'I am' (the full pronoun + enclitic -م gives the copular sense), هم = 'also/too' emphasizes inclusion, and سوار نبرد is a genitive/attributive phrase 'rider/horseman of battle' (i.e. a warrior). The clause as a whole: 'I too am a spahbed and a warrior in battle.'
segments.s210-g59: This noun phrase lacks an explicit copula (common in Persian): تو هم نامداری ز توران سپاه = 'you too are a famous man/from the host of Turan.' ز here is the poetic preposition 'from' (z + noun) and سپاه qualifies the source as 'the army/host.'
segments.s210-g60: چرا = 'why'. رای کردن is a classical expression 'to deliberate/decide' (رای = plan/counsel; کردن is the light verb), and کردی is second-person past 'you did/you made (the decision)'. به آوردگاه = 'to/into the battlefield' (به + آوردگاه). The whole asks why he resolved to come to the battlefield.
segments.s210-g61: دلت‌گر is the conditional construction 'دلَت + گر' = 'if your heart (should)...' (گر = 'if'); پذیرد is the present/subjunctive/optative form 'accept' (3rd singular) used in the protasis; یکی پند من = 'one (single) piece of my advice' — یکی quantifies پند (advice).
segments.s210-g62: بجویی is a subjunctive/imperative from جستن/جویی 'seek' (you should/seek). بدین is the fused form of به + این meaning 'in/with this' (here written as two tokens in the data), کار = 'matter/undertaking', and پیوند من = 'my bond/alliance/aid' (my support). Together: 'seek my alliance in this matter.' Note: the tokenized دین in the word list does not mean 'religion' here; it is part of the fused demonstrative (بدین = به + این).
segments.s210-g63: کزین = کز + این, an archaic/prepositional compound meaning 'from/for this' or 'because of this.' کینه = 'hatred/grudge.' تا زنده ماند = 'as long as (he/it) remains alive' (تا + verb). یکی here functions as 'one / even one' (i.e. 'while even one is alive').
segments.s210-g64: نیاسود is the negative perfective/stative 'did not rest / will not rest' (ن- prefix + آسود stem), and خواهد is the auxiliary/future marker 'will' here, producing 'will not rest.' سپاه is the subject 'the army/host' that follows the verb (typical flexible word order), and اندکی 'a little' with the negation gives the sense 'not even a little.'
segments.s210-g131: "ازان" (from that) introduces the source or result; "چاک چاک" is a reduplicated adjective meaning split/rent apart and intensifies the following noun; "عمودِ گران" is a noun phrase (عمود = pillar/shaft, گران = heavy). Together this phrase describes that, as a consequence of that tearing, the heavy shafts/pillars lay split.
segments.s210-g132: "سرانشان" is سران + the possessive suffix -شان, here meaning "their heads/tops." "چو" is the simile marker "like/as." "سندان آهنگران" is a genitive phrase meaning "the anvil of the blacksmiths" (سندان = anvil, آهنگران = blacksmiths). The whole clause is a simile: "their heads were like the blacksmiths' anvils."
segments.s210-g133: The sequence is locative + noun + verb: "بابر اندرون" = "within Babur" (name + اندرون "inside"). "بانگِ پولاد" is a genitive construction "the sound/clang of steel" (بانگ = sound/cry, پولاد = steel). "خاست" is the past verb "arose." So: "From within Babur the clang/sound of steel arose."
segments.s210-g134: Similarly structured: "بدریای" is the proper name Badrīyā. "شهدِ اندرون" groups sweetness/honey (شهد) with اندرون "within" to indicate an internal sweetness or honeyed quality; "باد خاست" means "a wind/breeze rose." The clause means "from within Badrīyā a honeyed/sweet breeze arose."
segments.s210-g135: "ز" (poetic preposition) introduces origin/agent: "ز خون" = "from/because of the blood." "بر کف شیر" = "on the paw/palm of the lion" (بر = on, کف = palm/paw, شیر = lion). "کفشیر بود" is the predicate "there was foam/froth (of milk)." The clause sets a vivid image: blood on the lion's paw and froth present.
segments.s210-g136: "همه دشت" = "the whole plain" (whole/entire + plain). "پر" here means "full (of)," and "بانگِ شمشیر" is the genitive "the sound/clamor of swords" (بانگ = sound, شمشیر = sword). "بود" is the past copula "was." The clause means "the entire plain was full of the clamor of swords."
segments.s210-g137: "خم آورد" is a verbal phrase literally "brought a bend" and idiomatically "bent/was bowed." "رویین عمودِ گران" is a noun phrase with modifiers: رویین = iron‑hard, عمود = pillar/shaft, گران = heavy. Word order places the action before the description, producing "the iron‑hard heavy pillar bent."
segments.s210-g138: "شد" = "became." "آهن" is the topic/subject "iron." "به کردار" = "into the likeness/form of" (به = into/towards, کردار here used as "form/shape"). "چاچی کمان" is a simile: چاچی (an older/dialectal word for a nurse/wet‑nurse) + کمان (bow). Thus: "the iron became in shape like a (wet‑)nurse's bow," i.e. it was bent into a bow shape. The phrasing is poetic/archaic.
segments.s210-g115: بدو گفت طوس is an inverted word order common in classical Persian: بدو (to him) + گفت (said) + طوس (Tūs) = “Tūs said to him.” این چه آشفتنست is a present-state question: این (this) + چه (what/how, exclamatory) + آشفتنست (poetic/archaic contraction of آشفتِه است / آشفته است, “is in disorder”).
segments.s210-g116: بدین دشت is a locative-demonstrative phrase: بدین (in/at this) + دشت (plain). پیکار تو با منست is a nominal clause with an archaic/fused copula: پیکار (the battle) + تو (you) + با (with) + منست (poetically contracted 'من است' / copula phrase) — meaning literally “the battle is with you and me,” i.e. concerns you and me.
segments.s210-g117: بیا is an imperative/hortative (“come” / “let us”). تا introduces a purpose/result clause here (‘so that’ / ‘that we may’). بگردیم is 1st-person-plural subjunctive/present (ب- prefix + گردیم) meaning “we turn/go about,” and و کین آوریم is a coordinate verb phrase: و (and) + کین (vengeance) + آوریم (1pl subjunctive ‘we bring’), i.e. “and bring vengeance.”
segments.s210-g118: بجنگ here functions as an hortative/locative marker ‘to battle’ / ‘let us fight.’ The following phrase is a poetic object/result: ابروان پر ز چین آوریم = ابروان (eyebrows) + پر (full) + ز (of/from) + چین (folds/wrinkles) + آوریم (we will bring/make). The verb آوریم is again 1st-person-plural subjunctive/optative paired with the hortative idea of بیا/بجنگ.
segments.s210-g119: بدو گفت هومان = “Houmān said to him” (بدو = to him, گفت = said, هومان = name). که introduces the reported content (‘that’). دادست is an archaic fused form (دادَست = داد + است / or gave/has given depending on context); here it functions as the verb introducing what death distributes: دادست مرگ = ‘death has given/deals’ or ‘death assigns.’
segments.s210-g120: This segment lists two parallel noun phrases governed by the verbal idea in the previous segment: سری زیر تاج (“a head under the crown”) و سری زیر ترگ (“and a head under rags”). سری = head, زیر = under/beneath, تاج = crown, ترگ = rag/shred; the coordinate structure makes a contrast (some heads under crowns, some under rags) with the verb implied from the previous clause.
segments.s210-g121: اگر introduces a conditional clause (“if”). مرگ باشد is a subjunctive/optative use of باشد (‘be’) referring to possession/lot (“if death be/of me”). مرا (split in the word list as م + را) is the object pronoun ‘me’ (مرا = ‘for me’ / ‘mine’). بی‌گمان is an adverb ‘undoubtedly’ modifying the whole conditional phrase.
segments.s210-g122: به آوردگه is a dative/locative phrase: به (to) + آوردگه (place of coming/assembly, ‘muster-place’). The second به is a stylistic repetition/emphasis before the temporal relative clause که آید زمان = ‘when the time comes’ (که = that/when, آید = comes, زمان = time). The clause as a whole says where/when death will take him.
segments.s210-g207: تو گفتی is a past tense 2nd-person form (you said). The sequence 'سپهر و زمان و زمین' lists three nouns joined by و (and) with normal coordinate order; in classical Persian the items are simply juxtaposed with 'و' between them rather than using any linking ezâfe. The whole segment is the reported subject of the following verb phrase.
segments.s210-g208: بپوشد is a 3rd-person present/subjunctive form (classical optative/contrary-to-fact sense: 'would/should cover' or 'will cover themselves'), with the prefixed b- marking the present/subjunctive in older Persian. همی is an archaic adverbial particle adding durative/affirmative force ('still/indeed' or marking habit/continuity). چادر آهنین is the object phrase 'an iron cloak/tent' (چادر here = cloak/veil rather than a literal tent). The verb takes the preceding coordinate nouns (سپهر، زمان، زمین) as its logical subjects.
segments.s210-g217: شما is the address ('you', plural or polite). یک بیک is a vocative or appositive noting 'one bey/chieftain' — classical Persian often places such appositions immediately after the vocative. تیغها is the plural direct object 'swords' and برکشید is the past form of برداشتن/برکشیدن meaning 'lifted/drew' (here: 'drew (their) swords'). Word order is relatively free; the verb comes at the end as in classical Persian.
segments.s210-g218: سپرهای چینی = 'Chinese shields' (سپرهای is the plural with the Persian plural suffix -ها and چینی qualifies it). The string 'بسر در کشید' is an older/poetic phrasing meaning 'drew (them) over/on the head' (بر سر → ب‍سر). در and کشید here operate together with بسر to express the motion of pulling/placing the shield in position; the lexical items are idiomatic in older Persian, so the particles combine with the verb to indicate 'drew over (their) heads.'
segments.s210-g219: مبینید is a present (you see / behold) in classical style; here addressed to the addressee(s). جز functions as the preposition/conjunction 'except' and introduces the remainder as the only things visible. یال اسپ is a genitive construction 'the mane of the steed' presented without an explicit ezâfe (common in epic diction), and عنان is 'the reins' listed in coordination with و.
segments.s210-g220: نشاید is an impersonal/jussive negative meaning 'it is not fitting' or 'ought not to be.' It governs the clause that follows. کمان is 'bow' and نباید is another impersonal negative modal 'must not / should not'; the final سنان is 'spear' (here the object/thing that should not be). The two clauses juxtapose: it is not fitting for the bow, and the spear likewise ought not to be used.
segments.s210-g221: عنان پاک = 'the clean/pure reins' (adjective following noun is normal in Persian). بر یال اسپان = 'upon the manes of the horses': بر = 'on', یال = 'mane', اسپان is the archaic plural of 'asp' (horse) formed with -ān. نهید is a classical present/subjunctive/imperative form of نهادن (to place) used here as an instruction 'place / set (you put)'.
segments.s210-g222: بدانسان که is an idiomatic compound meaning 'in that manner / so that.' The following verbs آید خورید و دهید form a result clause introduced by که; they are classical/poetic forms expressing the intended outcome. آید is 'comes' (3rd pers.), خورید here uses an older sense (a resultive/occurrent sense, roughly 'befalls/strikes' or 'takes effect') rather than the literal 'eat,' and دهید is 2nd-person present/subjunctive/imperative 'you give' (or 'you let/give'). Together they indicate the intended practical effect of setting the reins as instructed (the phrasing is idiomatic and compact in epic style).
segments.s210-g199: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. برزد is an archaic past/preterite verb meaning 'appeared/rose' used in V‑S (verb before its nominal patient) order typical of classical Persian poetry. سر 'head' is the noun acted on; از + برج خرچنگ is a prepositional phrase 'from the tower/sign of the Crab (Cancer)' giving origin, and شید follows as an epithet/appositive (poetic name/epithet).
segments.s210-g200: جهان گشت is a verbal clause 'the world became/turned' (گشت = became). چون is the comparative 'like/as', followed by the noun phrase روی رومی سپید where the adjective سپید follows the noun (typical Persian order) producing 'like the white face of a Roman.'
segments.s210-g201: تبیره برآمد is a nominal subject + verb: 'an interpretation/omen arose'. ز هر دو سرای is a source phrase 'from both sarā/households/quarters' (ز = from). Note: سرای here is poetic/archaic 'house/quarter' (the provided senses list relates to a verbal lemma; semantically it functions as a place noun).
segments.s210-g202: جهان شد پر از is the idiom 'the world became full of ...' (شد = became; پر از = full of). ناله کر نای is a poetic compound referring to plaintive sounds from wind instruments ('the lament/cry of the karnā (trumpet) and the nây (reed)'); note that classical Persian often strings instrument words together and can split compounds for metrical reasons.
segments.s210-g203: هوا تیره گشت is 'the air/atmosphere grew dark' (تیره گشت = became dark; گشت = became). از فروغ درفش is a causal/source phrase 'from the radiance/splendor of the standard/banner' (فروغ = radiance; درفش = banner), with از introducing the cause.
segments.s210-g204: This is a poetic noun/adjective list describing colours and images associated with the banner: طبر خون 'axe (or axe) and blood' and then a sequence of color words joined by و (and). In Persian poetry adjectives or color‑nouns often follow or coordinate without extra linking verbs, producing a catalogue of visual details.
segments.s210-g205: کشیده is a past participle/adjective 'drawn' (here governing the following weapons). The phrase follows with a coordinate list همه تیغ و گرز و سنان 'all (had) sword and mace and spear' — subject/agent is implied or general; word order (participle + list) is typical of epic narration.
segments.s210-g206: همه جنگ را گرد کرده عنان is a figurative clause: 'they have (literally) made the reins round/over the whole war' — i.e. 'taken control of the entire battle.' را marks the definite object (همه جنگ), کرده is the perfect participle/auxiliary 'having made/caused', گرد here conveys 'around/about' or 'gathered', and عنان ('reins') is used metaphorically for control/restraint.
segments.s210-g97: This is an exclamatory noun phrase: 'ایا' functions as an archaic vocative/exclamation addressing the idea that follows, 'گم‌شده بخت آزادگان' = 'the lost fortune of the free' (adjective before noun is allowed in poetic word order). 'گم‌شده' is a participial adjective 'lost'; 'بخت' here means fate/fortune; 'آزادگان' is a plural noun 'the free' (the noble/free-men).
segments.s210-g98: 'که' introduces a wish/optative clause here (roughly 'may it be that'), followed by the optative particle 'باد' attached to the verb/adjective 'گم' to mean 'may be lost'. The proper name 'گودرز' names the person wished to be lost; 'کشوادگان' is a lineage/tribal name in the plural. In short: 'که گم باد گودرز کشوادگان' = 'may Goudarz of the Keshvad line be lost'.
segments.s210-g99: 'فراوان' is an adverb here ('often/many times'). The sequence 'مرا دیده‌ای' is second-person singular perfective ('you have seen me' / 'you saw me'); object clitic and object marker are separated in the tokenization ('م' + 'را' in the data, but together form 'مرا' = 'me'). 'روز جنگ' is a time phrase 'on the day of battle' placed after the verb phrase (poetic word order).
segments.s210-g100: 'به آوردگه' is a locative/adverbial phrase 'at/to the place of assembly/muster'. 'تیغ هندی' is a noun + adjective 'Indian sword'. 'بچنگ' is the contracted poetic form of 'به چنگ' meaning 'in (one's) grasp / in hand'. The whole segment modifies the previous clause: 'at the muster, with an Indian sword in hand.'
segments.s210-g101: 'کس از تخم کشواد جنگی نماند' is a negative universal statement: 'کس ... نماند' = 'there remained no one'. 'از تخم' literally 'from the seed/of the offspring' = 'from the descendants of'; 'کشواد' is the ancestor/name; 'جنگی' is an adjective 'warlike/warrior'. So: 'No warrior remains from Keshvad's seed.'
segments.s210-g102: The relative clause introduced by 'که' ('who/that') qualifies the preceding 'کس': 'who did not ...'. 'منشور' here means 'charter/proclamation/inscribed document' associated metaphorically with the sword; 'تیغ' is 'sword'. The token 'م' in the data appears as a separate item but is actually the possessive clitic 'ـم' (= 'my') attached to 'تیغ' in the phrase 'تیغِ مرا' ('my sword'); 'را' is the object marker. 'برنخواند' is negative perfect/past 'did not read aloud' — so the clause: 'who did not read aloud the charter of my sword.'
segments.s210-g103: The phrase 'ترا بخت' (tokenized here as 'ت' + 'را') means 'your fortune' ('ترا' = 'to/for you' or possessive 'your' in older poetry). 'چون' = 'like/as'. 'روی آهرمنست' = 'the face/visage is Ahriman' — a metaphor saying the subject's luck resembles Ahriman's aspect. Note the archaic/poetic linking: the copula '-ست' attaches to 'آهرمن' as 'آهرمنست'.
segments.s210-g104: 'بخان' is an interjection ('bah!' — an expression of scorn or dismissal). 'تو' addresses 'you'. 'تا' = 'until' (here temporal extent), 'جاودان' = 'eternal'. 'شیونست' is a nominal/predicative construction meaning 'there is lamentation' or 'lamentation is (your lot)'. Together: 'Bah — for you there will be mourning forever.'
segments.s210-g139: تو گفتی که = 'you said that': تو is the 2nd-person pronoun and گفتی is a past-tense 2nd-person verb ('you said'). که is a complementizer introducing the reported clause. Inside the clause the predicate سنگ است ('is stone') precedes the subject سر (a poetic inversion): literally 'stone is head', i.e. 'the head is stone'. زیر ترگ is a prepositional phrase 'beneath the rag' modifying سر.
segments.s210-g140: سیه شد = 'became black': شد is the past form of شدن used here as 'became'. ز marks cause ('from / because of'), so ز زخم یلان = 'from the wounds of the warriors'. روی مرگ is a genitive construction (noun + noun) meaning 'the face/appearance of death' and describes the resulting look.
segments.s210-g141: گرفتند is past plural 'they seized/took'. شمشیر هندی is a noun + adjective ('Indian sword'), where the adjective هندی follows the noun. بچنگ is an adverbial/phrastic element meaning 'into the grasp / in hand', modifying گرفتند (i.e., they took it into their hands).
segments.s210-g142: فرو ریخت is a compound where فرو (down) precedes the verb ریخت ('poured/flowed'), giving 'poured down'. آتش is the subject/thing that poured; ز marks the source ('from steel and stone') with پولاد و سنگ as the sources. The verb is past tense and describes a sudden outburst of flame.
segments.s210-g143: ز نیروی گردنکشان = 'from the force/strength of the گردنکشان': ز marks cause/source, نیروی is the abstract noun 'force/strength', and گردنکشان is a descriptive plural (here 'the proud ones' / 'neck-breakers') that modifies نیروی. تیغ تیز ('sharp blade') is the noun + adjective phrase that will be the affected thing.
segments.s210-g144: خم آورد is a compound verbal expression meaning 'bent' (آورد used with خم to indicate 'caused/was made to bend' or simply 'bent'). The same subject then undergoes the result expressed by در زخم شد ریز ریز: در = 'in/inside', زخم = 'wound', شد = 'became/was', and the reduplicated ریز ریز emphasizes 'into very small/tiny pieces' (fractured/shattered).
segments.s210-g161: سپر بر سر آورد = 'he brought/held the shield over (his) head': سپر is the object, بر سر is a prepositional phrase 'upon/over the head', and آورد is past 'brought/raised'. و ننمود روی = 'and did not show his face': ننمود is the past negative of نمود ('show'), where the negative prefix ن- attaches to the past stem; روی is the object 'face'.
segments.s210-g162: نگه داشت is an idiom 'kept/held (a look, watch)': نگه = 'gaze/watch' and داشت here is the past auxiliary meaning 'kept/held (on doing)'; هومان is the proper name as subject. سر از تیر اوی is a verbless/elliptical phrase: سر 'head' + از 'away/from' + تیر اوی 'his arrow' (اوی as third-person possessive), meaning he kept his head away from (or safe from) his arrow.
segments.s210-g1: به‌اسپ is an adverbial prepositional phrase 'on horseback' placed before the subject. عقاب is the subject ('the eagle'); اندر آورد پای is a compound verbal expression (آوردن with اندر) meaning 'brought/put (his) foot' or idiomatically 'set foot/advanced'. The verb is past tense and the noun پای is the object/phrase completing the verbal idiom.
segments.s210-g2: برانگیخت is a past transitive verb 'he raised/uprooted'. آن بارگی را is the definite object (آن = 'that', بارگی = 'leaf/foliage', را marks it as the direct object). ز جای is an ablative phrase 'from its place' (ز = 'from', جای = 'place'). The clause therefore literally: 'he raised that foliage from its place.'
segments.s210-g3: تو is the 2nd-person subject 'you'. گفتی is the past form 'you said' (here the given sense list does not include the speech-verb sense, so that item is annotated as unmatched). یکی باره functions as an adverbial phrase 'in one instance' / 'in a single piece' and آهنست is a predicate 'is of iron' (آهن + -ست).
segments.s210-g4: وگر is a conditional/concessive 'but if' introducing a hypothetical. کوه البرز is the noun phrase 'the Alborz mountain' (proper name). در جوشنست is a predicative phrase: در = 'in' or 'as', جوشنست = 'is a cuirass/armour' (poetic copula -ست), so the clause reads 'if the Alborz mountain were a cuirass.'
segments.s210-g5: به پیش سپاه is a locative phrase 'before the army/host' (به ... پیش = towards/in front of), اندر آمد is the past-tense verb 'he came in/advanced', and بجنگ (ب + جنگ) is an adverbial/infinitival phrase 'into battle' or 'to fight' complementing آمد. The whole means 'he advanced before the host into the fight.'
segments.s210-g6: یکی is the indefinite/number 'one' or 'a certain'. خشت is the noun 'brick/slab' used poetically; رخشان is a postpositive adjective 'shining/radiant'. گرفته is a perfect participle 'having taken/seized' used adjectivally, and بچنگ (ب + چنگ) is a fused adverbial phrase 'into the grasp / in the hand'. Together: 'he had seized a shining brick in his hand.'
segments.s210-g7: بجنبید is an epic/poetic past form of جنبیدن here meaning 'was roused/moved' (3rd person). طوس سپهبد is an appositive noun phrase 'Tūs, the Spahbed' (title after the name). ز جای is the ablative 'from his place.' The clause therefore: 'Tūs the Spahbed was roused from his place.'
segments.s210-g8: جهان is the subject 'the world'. پر شد is a two-word predicate 'became full / was filled' (پر = 'full (of)', شد = 'became'). از introduces the cause/contents 'with/from', ناله = 'moan/laments', کر qualifies نای (the following noun) and here functions adjectivally as 'deaf' or 'deafening' applied to the flute, and نای is 'the reed/ney (flute)'. Literally: 'the world became full from the reed's deafening moan.'
