segments.s210-g113: تو is the vocative/subject 'you.' اکنون is the temporal adverb 'now.' بمرگ برادر‌گری is a prepositional/verbal-nominal phrase meaning 'at the death of brotherhood' or 'with brotherhood dying' (بمرگ = 'to/toward death', برادرگری = 'brotherhood/fraternal bond'), describing the present critical situation in which the addressee is asked to act.
segments.s210-g114: چه is the interrogative 'how/what' here asking for manner. با introduces the parties in dispute ('between/with') and is followed by the two proper names طوس and نوذر (Tūs and Nowzar). کنی is the 2nd-person present/subjunctive form of 'کردن' used to ask 'what will you do / how will you act,' and داوری is the noun 'judgment/arbitration' serving as the object: 'How will you render judgment between Tūs and Nowzar?'
segments.s210-g89: فریبنده is an epithet ('deceptive' or 'deceiver') placed before the proper name هومان for poetic effect: 'Homan the deceiver' or 'the deceitful Homan'. میان دو صف is a locative prepositional phrase meaning 'between the two ranks/lines' (میان = 'between', صف = 'battle line/formation').
segments.s210-g90: بیامد is the simple past 'he came'. دمان (here from دامن, 'hem/skirt') with بر لب آورده کف is a participial phrase: آورده is a past participle 'having brought', followed by the noun کف ('palm'), so the whole phrase describes a demeanour/gesture — 'having brought (his) palm to (his) lips' (بر لب = 'to/on the lip'). Word order is poetic: participle + noun describes manner of arrival.
segments.s210-g91: کنون 'now' sets the temporal frame. با تو 'with you' marks the addressee. چندین is an intensifying/adverbial word ('many/much' or 'for a long time'). چه گوید is an interrogative phrase 'what will (he) say' where گوید functions as 'say' (here the lemma list gave 'conversation' but the syntactic role is verbal 'say'); براز appears as the subject or name (here printed as براز but context/meaning is ambiguous — the lemma list only gives 'dung', so this word may be a nickname or epithet).
segments.s210-g92: میان دو صف is again the locative 'between the two ranks'. گفت و گوی is the compound noun 'گفت و گو' ('conversation' or 'dialogue') and دراز is a postpositive adjective 'long' — together 'a long conversation' (i.e. a lengthy exchange took place between the lines).
segments.s210-g93: سخن is 'word' or 'speech' placed at the front for emphasis. جز introduces the exception 'except'. بشمشیر is a fused prepositional phrase meaning 'by the sword' (بـ + شمشیر). با او 'with him' marks the interlocutor. مگوی is the negative imperative 'do not say' (second-person sing.). The clause literally commands: 'Say no speech to him except by the sword.'
segments.s210-g94: مجوی is another negative imperative (do not seek). از here is 'from'/'of' and در means 'door/gate' (i.e. 'the gate of ...'); آشتی is 'reconciliation' or 'truce'. هیچ روی is an idiomatic negation meaning 'in no way' or 'at all' (هیچ = 'no/none', روی = 'face/manner' used poetically). Altogether: 'Seek no (thing) from the gate of reconciliation in any manner' → 'do not seek reconciliation at all.'
segments.s210-g95: چو is the conjunction 'when'. بشنید is the simple past 'he heard'. هومان is the subject. برآشفت is a simple past/inchoative verb 'rose up in anger' / 'became enraged'. سخت is an adverbial intensifier here ('very/violently/strongly'). The clause reads: 'When Homan heard, he was greatly angered.'
segments.s210-g96: چنین is an adverb 'thus/so'. گفت here functions as past 'he said' (the lemma senses list gave the noun 'conversation', so that sense did not cover the verbal use and is marked accordingly). با گیو is the prepositional phrase 'to/with Giv' (addressed). بیدار بخت is a compound epithet: بیدار 'awake/alert' modifying بخت 'fortune/fate' — a direct form of address ('O wakeful-fortune' or an epithet applied to Giv).
segments.s210-g163: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when'. او را uses the object marker را after the pronoun او (him) — in older/narrative Persian the object may be topicalized with را even before the verb. پیاده = 'on foot / dismounted'. بران is a demonstrative/adverbial meaning 'on that / thereon', and رزمگاه is the noun 'battlefield'. The whole phrase is a temporal subordinate clause: 'When they saw him dismounted there on the battlefield...'.
segments.s210-g164: بدیدند is the simple past 'they saw'. گفتند would normally be 'they said', but here the provided sense list did not include that basic sense (see note). The noun phrase توران سپاه follows the verbs: classical/persian epic word order often places verbs before the subject, so 'توران سپاه' is the subject ('the Turanian host'). Together: 'they saw ... and the Turanian host said/uttered (something)'.
segments.s210-g165: که introduces a subordinate/explanatory clause ('that' / 'so that'). پردخت here is a noun meaning an ornament/embellishment (classical spelling without alef); ماند is past 'remained/was left'. کنون = 'now'. جای اوی = 'in his place' (جای = place/position; اوی = 'his' or 'of him' in older usage). The clause states that some piece of accoutrement or payment remained in his place.
segments.s210-g166: ببردند is past plural 'they took / carried away' (ب‑ prefix + بردند). پرمایه functions as an adjective meaning 'rich / substantial'. بالای literally 'over / on top of' and here marks the locus from which things were taken; اوی is the possessive 'his' (older form). The clause means 'they took away his rich goods from above him / from atop him'.
segments.s210-g167: چو again = 'when'. هومان is the proper name Hūmān. بران = 'thereon / on that', and زین is 'saddle'. توزی is an archaic/epic epithet or adjective attached to زین (a type or name of saddle) — its exact lexical sense is obscure in the supplied list, so it should be read as a descriptive element modifying saddle. نشست is past 'sat' (he sat/settled). The whole segment gives a temporal clause: 'When Hūmān sat on that (Tūzī) saddle...'.
segments.s210-g168: یکی = 'one' used as 'someone / a certain person'. تیغ = 'sword'. بگرفت = past 'he seized / took hold of' (ب‑ prefix + گرفت). هندی here qualifies the sword as 'Indian' (an Indian-made blade) and بدست is an adverbial 'in hand' (literally 'in the hand'), so the phrase describes someone seizing an Indian sword, holding it in his hand.
segments.s210-g169: که here introduces the clause (roughly 'that' / 'when'). آید is present/habitual form 'comes' but in epic narrative it can be used to mean 'came' in the sense of a repeated or timeless action. دگر باره is a compound meaning 'again / once more' (دگر = again, باره = occasion/instance). بر جنگ = 'to the battle' (بر = toward/to). طوس is the name Tūs (here the hero), the subject of آید. The clause: 'when Tūs came again to the battle'.
segments.s210-g170: شد is the past 'became'. از شب = 'from the night' or 'with the onset of night' (از = from; شب = night). جهان تیره = 'the world (became) dark' (تیره = dark/ominous). چون آبنوس = 'like ebony' (simile). The clause describes the world's darkness being as black as ebony when night fell.
segments.s210-g41: بفرمای is a polite imperative/command meaning 'order' or 'bid' and here introduces a subordinate clause with تا. تا + verb_clause expresses purpose or result ('so that / that'). آورند is a 3rd-person plural classical form (subjunctive/hortative/historical present) placed at the end of the clause, so جنگ شیر آورند = 'that they bring a fighting/lion contest' (i.e. 'bring fighting lions'). Note Persian word order: conjunction تا + (object + modifier) + verb at the end.
segments.s210-g42: زبردست را is a definite/direct-object phrase (را marks the definite object: 'the mighty' or 'the powerful one'). The predicate دست زیر آورند is an idiomatic verbal phrase: دست زیر آوردن means 'to bring under one's hand' — i.e. to subdue or make submissive. Here آورند is again 3pl, so the whole line reads 'they put the powerful one under (their) hand / they humbled the mighty.'
segments.s210-g43: اگر introduces a conditional clause. شوی here is 2nd-person singular of شدن used with the past participle کشته to form a passive/result ('you become killed' = 'you are killed'). The phrase بر دست من ('on/by my hand') gives the agent/instrument: Persian often uses بر + دست + agent to mean 'by the hand of X.' Word order: subject (تو) + verb (شوی) + participle (کشته) + prepositional phrase (بر دست من).
segments.s210-g44: بد آید is an impersonal/experiential construction meaning 'it will be bad/unfavorable' or 'it will be regrettable.' آید is the (classical) present/future sense 'happens/will come.' بدان here functions as a dative-like expression 'to that' (classical ב- prefix + demonstrative) and names the perspective; نامدار انجمن 'renowned assembly' is the dative/experiencer: 'it will seem bad to that famed assembly.' This is a literary order where the verb phrase precedes the dative recipient.
segments.s210-g45: سپاه تو is the noun + possessor (your army). Adjectival predicates بی‌یار و بیجان ('friendless and lifeless') follow the noun, and شوند is the 3rd-person plural of شدن used as 'become' or 'will be.' The structure is straightforward: subject → adjectival predicate → copular verb (they become).
segments.s210-g46: اگر introduces a conditional clause. زنده مانند here means 'as if alive' or 'while alive like' (مانند = 'like/as'). پیچان is an active/adjectival participle meaning 'twisting/writhing' (here describing the manner), and شوند is again 3rd-plural 'they will become' / 'they will be' used to complete the conditional result. Word order places manner modifier before the verb.
segments.s210-g47: و دیگر = 'and furthermore / moreover.' که‌گر (classical conditional 'if') introduces the subordinate clause; بشنوی is 2nd-person singular present/subjunctive meaning 'you hear' or 'you be told.' گفت here is used as a noun 'saying' or 'utterance' (what is said), and راست modifies it as 'true' — so the clause is 'if you hear another true saying' (literally 'if you hear another say true'). Note the compact classical ordering where the verb of perception (بشنوی) can precede the noun phrase of what is heard.
segments.s210-g48: روان و دلم = 'my spirit and my heart' (روان = soul/spirit; دل = heart). بر زبانم = 'upon my tongue' (بر = on/upon; زبانم = 'my tongue' or 'my speech'); گواست here is a literary/elliptical form meaning 'is witness' or 'bear witness.' The construction asserts that both spirit and heart testify by/through the speaker's tongue. Note that some classical forms and affixes are split in the lemma list (e.g., دلم shown as دل + م), but here they function as simple possessive phrases.
segments.s210-g123: بدست is a prepositional/agentive phrase here meaning 'into the hand of / by the hand of' placed before its noun (poetic order). سواری is the noun 'rider/horseman' and is followed by a relative clause introduced by که + دارد ('who has'); هنر is the direct object of دارد and means 'skill'.
segments.s210-g124: This is a coordinated nominal phrase (three nouns joined by و). سپهبدسر is an epithet/title 'chief commander'; و is the coordinating 'and'; گرد here functions as another epithet/term in the list; the final و + پرخاشخر gives a third epithet meaning roughly 'aggressive / quarrelsome'. The whole phrase labels who or what the skilled rider affects.
segments.s210-g125: گرفتند is the past plural verb 'they seized/took'. هر دو is the phrase 'both' (هر here contributes the distributive/collective sense), followed by عمود گران 'heavy pillar(s)'; adjective گران follows the noun in typical Persian order and qualifies عمود.
segments.s210-g126: همی here is an emphatic/progressive particle used in older/poetic Persian to mark the immediacy or continuation of the action ('they began/kept on'). حمله is the noun 'assault/attack'. برد in this construction functions with حمله to mean 'launched/made' (poetic usage). The final string آن برین این بران uses correlative demonstratives and directional particles: آن ('that') and این ('this') combined with بر(+ -ین/ـان) to indicate 'against/on that side, on this side' — i.e. one attack toward one side, the other toward the other side.
segments.s210-g127: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' or 'because of' starting the clause. می‌گشت is the imperfective/progressive form 'was turning/was wheeling' (می‑ prefix marks ongoing action in older Persian). گردان is the subject 'the troop/company'. و شد روز تار is coordinate: شد 'became' + روز 'day' + تار 'dark' (tār used poetically to mean 'dark'), so 'and the day became dark.'
segments.s210-g128: یکی + ابر = 'a cloud' (one cloud). بست is the past of بستن here meaning 'closed up / drew together / gathered' (about clouds). از بر کارزار is a prepositional phrase 'over/on the battlefield' (از بر = 'over / above' in older poetic usage), so the clause means 'a cloud drew together over the battlefield.'
segments.s210-g129: تو گفتی is the second-person past 'you said' (گفتی is 2nd-singular past, despite the lemma list showing 'conversation' — here it is the verb). شب آمد بریشان is reported speech or content: شب آمد 'night came' with بریشان (بر + ـشان) meaning 'upon them' or 'to them'. بروز here means 'in the daytime / by day' or 'at the appearance (of day)' depending on context; the clause reads as a reported remark about night coming upon them by day.
segments.s210-g130: نهان گشت is a two-word predicate 'became hidden' (نهان = hidden, گشت = became/turned). خورشید گیتی فروز is an appositive noun phrase 'the sun, the world's-illuminator' (گیتی فروز = 'that which lights the world'); the phrase as a whole says that the world-illuminating sun was hidden/turned away.
segments.s210-g179: ز تو = 'from you' (prepositional phrase placed before the noun); خشم گردنکشان = 'the anger (wrath) of the گردنکشان' (possessive relationship expressed by simple juxtaposition in poetry); دور باد uses باد as an optative particle meaning 'may it be far'. The overall clause is a wish: 'May the rebels' anger be far from you.'
segments.s210-g180: درین = در + این (archaic fused form) meaning 'in this'; جنگ = 'battle'; فرجام ما = 'the outcome/end belonging to us' (possessive follows the noun); سور باد again uses باد as an optative: 'may (it be) a feast'. The clause means 'In this battle may our outcome be a feast (i.e. joyful).'
segments.s210-g181: که introduces the clause and functions here as a literary connector; چون بود = 'how was' with چون = 'how' and بود = past tense 'was'; رزم تو = 'your battle/fight'; تو ای نامجوی is a vocative ('you, O Namajuy') appended to the clause. Word order is inverted for poetic effect.
segments.s210-g182: چو = 'when'; با = 'with' (accompanying/against); طوس = Tous (the hero) here; روی = 'face' (i.e. 'in face of'); اندر آمد = 'came in/approached' (compound of اندر + آمد); بروی = infinitive/verb 'to go/approach' used here to indicate movement toward encounter. Together: 'when you came face to face with Tous.'
segments.s210-g183: همه پاک is an emphatic adverbial 'all/entirely' (پاک used adverbially = 'utterly/completely'); ما = subject 'we'; دل پر از خون = 'heart(s) full of blood' (پر از = 'full of'); بدیم here is a predicative meaning 'we are in a bad/ill state' — an archaic/poetic adjectival use meaning 'we fare badly'.
segments.s210-g184: جز ایزد = 'except God' (short for 'none except God'); نداند is the literary present/past-form of 'to know' (here 'knows'); که introduces the subordinate clause 'that/how'; ما چون بدیم = 'how we are/what state we are in' (چون = 'how', بدیم = 'are ill/faring poorly'). The line says: 'Only God knows how we fare.'
segments.s210-g17: بجنگ is a purpose/locative phrase (به جنگ) meaning 'to/for battle' and appears before the subject and verb in this poetic order; تو is the addressed second-person pronoun (you); آیم is first-person singular present/subjunctive/formal-future 'I come' or 'I will come' placed after the object phrase; بسان + noun is a simile 'like/as a ...' modifying the manner of the coming (آیم).
segments.s210-g18: که introduces a relative clause modifying 'پلنگ' ('which/that'); از کوه is a simple source phrase 'from the mountain'; یازد is an archaic verbal form (3rd sg.) here meaning 'breaks forth / rushes out' (poetic for 'leaps'); بنخچیر 'prey' and چنگ 'claw' stand in close apposition — the clause says the leopard rushes out of the mountain with its claw upon the prey (poetic, somewhat compact word order).
segments.s210-g19: ببینی is a second-person form of دیدن used here as 'you will see' (or 'you shall see'); the pronoun تو is explicit 'you'; پیکار is 'battle/fight' and is followed by a genitive/attributive phrase مردانِ مرد ('men of men' = 'the mightiest men' — a poetic intensifier). The noun order (head then modifier) is a frequent poetic arrangement.
segments.s210-g20: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'; آورد is 3rd-person classical/narrative 'brings/comes' ('when it/that comes'); گیرم is 1st-person present/subjunctive or jussive 'I seize / I will seize' (used for a future action); بدشت نبرد is a locative phrase 'to the plain of battle' (بدشت 'plain' followed by نبرد specifying which plain). The clause expresses 'when X happens, I will Y (to the battlefield)'.
segments.s210-g21: چنین is an adverb 'thus/so' placed up front; پاسخ is the direct object 'answer/reply'; آورد is the verb 'brought/returned/said' (classical narrative past) and the proper name هومان follows it in typical SOV/poetic order; بدوی is an epithet attached to the name (here treated as an adjective or epithet after the name).
segments.s210-g22: که introduces the reported speech or content clause 'that...'; بیشی نه خوبست literally 'more not is good' = 'more is not good' (negation via نه before the predicate); مجوی is the negative imperative/subjunctive 'do not seek' (addressing the listener). The second بیشی repeats 'more' for emphasis; subject ('you') is omitted and understood.
segments.s210-g23: گر is the conditional 'if'; ایدونک is a demonstrative/particle ('such/so' — poetic/old usage) modifying the following noun; بیچاره‌ای is 'an unfortunate one/a poor wretch' and را is the direct-object/definiteness marker attached to it; زمان ('time') sets the temporal frame — together this yields 'if at such a time an unfortunate one...'.
segments.s210-g24: بدست تو آمد is an idiomatic phrase 'comes into your hand / falls into your power' (بدست = 'into the hand / into the power of', آمد = 'came'); مشو is the negative imperative 'do not become' (here elliptically 'do not be'); در گمان is a locative/idiomatic phrase 'in suspicion/doubt' — together 'do not be in doubt/suspicious'.
